attributes.stem:
A 27-year-old woman, G1P0, presents to the Emergency Department with a 24 hour history of increasing right upper quadrant pain associated with nausea. She is normally in good health, with no significant medical or surgical history. She is currently 30 weeks pregnant.

On examination she looks well, with a blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg, pulse 90/min and temperature 37.6C. She is tender immediately under the right costal margin. The rest of the abdomen is soft and the findings consistent with a 30 week pregnancy. An ultrasound confirms the presence of gallstones in a thick-walled gallbladder.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following management options would be most appropriate?
options: Admit for antibiotics and intravenous therapy and conservative management
Defer cholecystectomy until after delivery of the baby
attributes.explanation:
It was previously taught that semi-elective abdominal surgery should be delayed until the postpartum period due to the perceived risk of surgical and obstetric complications (including preterm labour) however this approach is associated with increased patient discomfort, lower patient satisfaction and increased presentations to hospital for management of the same symptoms. Whilst it is still not uncommon to see pregnant women with an acute abdomen related to acute cholecystitis/ recurrent cholelithiasis managed conservatively on the ward, there are a multitude of studies supporting the safety of laparoscopic surgery for cholecystitis and appendicitis in pregnancy. Laparoscopic surgery into the third trimester has been shown to be safe with improved patient outcomes, high levels of patient satisfaction and low risk to the fetus when compared to conservative management. Hence, a laparoscopic cholecystectomy should be undertaken. Admission and informing the obstetric team is part of offering multidisciplinary care and a midwife or resident can perform a fetal heart rate pre and post operatively.

Percutaneous cholecystostomy, the ultrasound guided placement of a drainage catheter into the gallbladder lumen, whilst being minimally invasive is not the management option for a fit and well woman who will have a better outcome and significantly lower complication rate from a laparoscopic approach. Deferring the procedure for two weeks will delay management with an increased risk of recurrence and the same can be said for delaying the procedure to the postpartum period where elective surgery will separate mother and newborn baby and impact breastfeeding.

Despite studies showing this, most women in the United States who develop cholecystitis during pregnancy are still managed non-operatively. This practice has led to higher rates symptom recurrent and repeated re-admissions and increased rates of maternal-fatal complications.
learningPoints: Laparoscopic surgery performed during the third trimester of pregnancy is safe.
references: Rios-Diaz AJ, Oliver EA, Bevilacqua LA, et al. Is It Safe to Manage Acute Cholecystitis Nonoperatively During Pregnancy? Ann Surg 2020; 272:449-56
Weinstein MS, Feuerwerker S, Baxter JK. Appendicitis and Cholecystitis in Pregnancy. Clin Obstet Gynecol. 2020;63(2):405-415. doi:10.1097/GRF.0000000000000529

Weiner E, Mizrachi Y, Keidar R, Kerner R, Golan A, Sagiv R. Laparoscopic surgery performed in advanced pregnancy compared to early pregnancy. Arch Gynecol Obstet. 2015;292(5):1063-1068. doi:10.1007/s00404-015-3744-8

Kwon H, Lee M, Park HS, Yoon SH, Lee CH, Roh JW. Laparoscopic management is feasible for nonobstetric surgical disease in all trimesters of pregnancy. Surg Endosc. 2018;32(6):2643-2649. doi:10.1007/s00464-018-6189-x
Gilo NB, Amini D, Landy HJ. Appendicitis and cholecystitis in pregnancy. Clin Obstet Gynecol. 2009;52(4):586-596. doi:10.1097/GRF.0b013e3181c11d10

Jorge AM, Keswani RN, Veerappan A, Soper NJ, Gawron AJ. Non-operative management of symptomatic cholelithiasis in pregnancy is associated with frequent hospitalizations. J Gastrointest Surg. 2015;19(4):598-603. doi:10.1007/s11605-015-2757-8

Loozen Charlotte S, van Santvoort Hjalmar C, van Duijvendijk Peter, Besselink Marc GH, Gouma Dirk J, Nieuwenhuijzen Grard AP et al. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy versus percutaneous catheter drainage for acute cholecystitis in high risk patients (CHOCOLATE): multicentre randomised clinical trial BMJ 2018; 363 :k3965
